query: association between lower fasting plasma glucose at T2D diagnosis and mortality/complications

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In a 15-year study of 1939 patients with type 2 diabetes, patients with fasting plasma glucose equal to or greater than 140 mg/dL showed a significantly increased risk of death. [80] Similarly, type 2 diabetic patients with fasting plasma glucose equal to or greater than 140 mg/dL were found to have increased cardiovascular mortality. [81] Other studies also report the risk of a first myocardial infarction increases with higher fasting plasma glucose levels. [82]

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Patients at the borderline of these criteria for DM are considered prediabetic. [66] The ADA criteria define prediabetes as impaired fasting glucose (IFG) defined as fasting plasma glucose of 100 to 125 mg/dL or impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) 2-hour plasma glucose (in OGTT) of 140 to 199 mg/dL or A1c of 5.7% to 6.4%. Patients with prediabetes are at high risk of progressing to DM. In particular, a systematic review found that increased A1c from 6% to 6.5% correlated with an increased 5-year incidence of DM. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Prediabetes is not a clinical entity itself; however, it often serves to identify people at increased risk of developing diabetes and cardiovascular disease. [5] Prediabetes is diagnosed using one of the three methods: Fasting plasma glucose 100 to 126 mg/dl A 2-hr postprandial glucose 140 to 199 mg/dl after 75 gm glucose load Hemoglobin A1C 5.7 to 6.4% Some entities describe prediabetes as fasting plasma glucose between 110 to 126 mg/dl. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Measurements of plasma glucose levels are essential for screening metabolic dysregulation, pre-diabetes, and diabetes. Evidence finds that the onset of type 2 diabetes can occur as early as 4 to 7 years before clinical diagnosis. Additionally, plasma glucose levels can monitor diabetes, screen for hypoglycemic episodes, guide treatment or lifestyle interventions, and predict risk for comorbidities like cardiovascular or eye and kidney disease. [78] In particular, evidence reports that plasma glucose level monitoring in patients with type 2 diabetes can predict complications and mortality. Higher glucose correlates with more significant comorbidities and risk for mortality. [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes can be diagnosed in either of four ways: Random hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) ≥6.5% Fasting blood sugar ≥126 mg/dl A 2-hr postprandial glucose ≥200 mg/dl after a 75 gm standard oral glucose load Random blood sugar ≥200 mg/dl in a patient with classic symptoms of diabetes (polyuria, polydipsia, and/or polyphagia) or in the patient presenting with a hyperglycemic crisis

retrieved_docs.title: Prediabetes -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Many studies have sown that there is a relationship between persistently elevated blood glucose levels and risk for adverse cardiac events and death. Evidence shows that individuals with prediabetes are susceptible to many metabolic complications that may lead to blindness, stroke, heart disease, and renal failure. These individuals are also at a high risk for developing peripheral neuropathy and loss of limb. Further, the elevation of blood glucose during pregnancy also increase the risk of maternal and fetal mortality. [15] [4] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: MODY 2 (GCK) patients generally have a good prognosis as a result of the relatively mild hyperglycemia and low complication rates. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 2 Diabetes -- Evaluation -- Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG)
retrieved_docs.content: A blood sample is taken after an 8 hour overnight fast. As per ADA, fasting plasma glucose (FPG) level of more than 126 mg/dL (7.0 mm/L) is consistent with the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Intolerance -- Evaluation -- Fasting plasma glucose (FPG)
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia due to deficiency of iron or vitamin B12 leads to spurious elevation of A1C, limiting its use in countries with high prevalence of anemia. Also, in children and elderly, the relation between A1C and FPG is suboptimal [5] .

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Any of the first three standard tests can be used to screen for type 1 or type 2 diabetes. For gestational diabetes, an oral glucose tolerance test is used with different glucose load and cut-off values than in non-pregnant adults. When HbA1c is being measured, it is noteworthy that some hemoglobin variants can interfere with certain essays of HbA1c and should be taken into account. If the patient was diagnosed with diabetes using lab testing (HbA1c or fasting/postprandial blood sugar) without having clinical symptoms, it is essential to send a second test to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of DM has serious consequences. DM confers a risk of long-term diabetic complications, including blindness, renal failure, and amputations, as well as an increased risk of cardiovascular disease. The condition also entails a lifetime of dietary restrictions and medications and can seriously curtail lifestyle and employment prospects. [37] The diagnosis may be suggested by the patient’s history or by the results of dipstick tests for glucose on urine specimens. However, urine glucose measurements are inadequate for diagnosing DM. [63] They potentially yield false-positive results in subjects with a low renal threshold for glucose. A patient with DM may deliver false-negative results if the patient is fasting. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Data and evidence from large observational studies have shown a positive association between glycemia measurements, including hemoglobin A1C and fasting glucose levels, with the risk associated with cardiovascular disease. Specifically, these studies have shown that hyperglycemia is a modifiable risk factor for coronary artery disease and atherosclerotic disease. In a meta-analysis of 10 independent studies involving the measurement of hemoglobin A1C in individuals with diabetes and cardiovascular disease, it was discovered that the relative risk for total cardiovascular disease and individuals with type 2 diabetes was 1.18 for each one percent increase in glycosylated hemoglobin, indicating an increase cardiovascular risk with increasing hyperglycemia. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity and Type 2 Diabetes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Many healthcare quality metrics assess the effectiveness of diabetes prevention and treatment. Obesity is a major risk factor for T2D, influencing both its development and severity. Early screening of patients with obesity, combined with intensive treatment, can lead to long-term improvement or remission. Strict diabetes control reduces complications such as ketoacidosis, diabetic ulcers, amputations, soft tissue infections, and osteomyelitis. Additionally, aggressive management of HbA1c lowers the risk of coronary artery disease and chronic kidney disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There is compelling evidence that early treatment of diabetes can reduce microvascular complications of diabetes. [15] The prompt treatment of the disease has significantly improved mortality, especially related to vascular disease. [5] The evidence for the impact of screening for diabetes is increasing. It is considered to be 1 of the more straightforward ways to prevent and reduce morbidity and mortality of this disease. Excess mortality related to type 2 diabetes results from cardiovascular events, and early screening has the potential to allow for early intervention. The UKPDS demonstrated a 14% reduction in myocardial infarctions for every 1% decrease in HbA1c value. [16] Gestational diabetes is an identified cause of several adverse outcomes during pregnancy, like pre-eclampsia, macrosomia, birth trauma, perinatal mortality, and neonatal cardiac, respiratory, and metabolic complications. [17] There is also a long-term risk of developing type 1/type 2 diabetes mellitus and cardiovascular disease in the mother, as well as metabolic syndrome, obesity, hypertension, and impaired glucose tolerance in the child. Identifying this early and prompt management has a positive impact on the health of the mother as well as the child. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 2 Diabetes -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: DM is associated with increased atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) and treating blood pressure, statin use, regular exercise, and smoking cessation are of great importance in ameliorating risk. The overall excess mortality in those with T2DM is around 15% higher but varies widely. The prevalence of vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy in the United States is about 4.4% among adults with diabetes, while it is 1% for end-stage renal disease. Today, with pharmacotherapy for hyperglycemia, as well as lowering LDL cholesterol and managing blood pressure with ACE/ARB therapy, with other antihypertensive medications and aspirin in secondary prevention, vascular complications can be managed adequately, resulting in a reduction in morbidity and mortality. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Definition/Introduction -- Type 2 Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Type 2 diabetes may initially have few symptoms; however, long-term effects lead to a myriad of different problems late in the course of the disease, leading to debilitating sequelae. More than 30 million people in the US have diabetes. [3] Due to an initial silent course, it often remains undetected for quite a long time before the appearance of symptoms. The screening for the disease aims at prevention and early treatment with the assumption that there is a long-term benefit to its identification early in the disease process. The long-term benefit of screening vs. clinically detected diabetes is being explored. [4] Several studies with weak evidence suggest the merits of screening tests. [5] Screening for type 2 diabetes can be done by fasting blood sugar, hemoglobin (A1C), glucose tolerance testing, and random plasma sugar. Urine glucose may be useful, but it is an inferior test to screen for diabetes, although research is reconsidering this claim. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

This integrated understanding emphasizes the importance of comprehensive management strategies that address both the primary metabolic disorder and its extensive systemic manifestations.

Overall, the interrelationships among the insulin‑receptor target, insulin aspart formulations, diabetes subtypes, hypoglycemia, related endocrine disorders, and associated gastrointestinal manifestations highlight the importance of integrated patient management to optimize glycemic control while avoiding adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), indicating that elevated blood cholesterol levels frequently coexist with this form of diabetes. This relationship underscores the importance of simultaneous screening for lipid abnormalities in patients with type 2 diabetes and integrated management of both cholesterol and glycemic control to improve overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
retrieved_docs.content: High plasma osmolality, high blood glucose, acute kidney failure, type 2 diabetes, an abnormal psychological state, and low blood volume are closely interrelated clinical entities. High plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels, reflecting the hyperosmolar consequences of uncontrolled diabetes, and it is also linked to type 2 diabetes itself, indicating that patients with this form of diabetes frequently develop increased plasma osmolality. In addition, high plasma osmolality is clinically associated with an abnormal psychological state, consistent with the neurocognitive disturbances that can arise from severe hyperosmolar conditions, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that dehydration or hypovolemia often accompanies the hyperosmolar state. Acute kidney failure is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes, underscoring diabetes as a major risk factor for rapid loss of renal function, and it is also linked to an abnormal psychological state, reflecting the mental status changes that can result from uremic toxins. Moreover, acute kidney failure is clinically associated with low blood volume, indicating that hypovolemia can precipitate or worsen renal injury. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which type 2 diabetes can lead to high plasma osmolality and acute kidney failure, both of which are connected to low blood volume and neuropsychiatric disturbances, emphasizing the importance of monitoring osmolar status, volume balance, renal function, and mental health in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with these forms of diabetes are at increased risk for developing sudden renal impairment. Dehydration (التجفاف) is also clinically linked to acute kidney failure, serving as a common precipitating factor that can exacerbate renal dysfunction in diabetic individuals. Recognizing the interplay among acute kidney failure, diabetes (both type 1 and insulin‑dependent type 2), and dehydration is essential for early identification, prevention, and management of renal complications in these patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2046
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES encompasses a spectrum of related conditions. It includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which denotes elevated blood glucose levels, and مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, representing diabetes without complications. Neurological involvement is reflected by اعتلال عصبي سكري, a diabetic neuropathy. Facial manifestations may appear as ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive hemi‑facial atrophy that can be associated with pancreatic endocrine disorders. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by فرط أنسولين الدم, an excess of circulating insulin, and by ورم غلوكاغوني, a glucagon‑producing tumor of the pancreas. Together these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations and complications that arise from diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1919
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism include the SMQ titled “ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد” (new‑onset diabetes mellitus with elevated blood glucose), and this SMQ in turn comprises the clinical effect “تأثير إدرار البول” (increased urine output). This relationship indicates that new‑onset diabetes, classified within lipid metabolism disorders, is associated with the manifestation of polyuria, highlighting the interconnected nature of metabolic dysregulation and its renal‑related symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2614
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is described as a general clinical state that is interpreted by clinicians (is_interpreted_by) and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications. This indicates that an uncontrolled disease condition determination reflects a concerning overall clinical status and frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with such uncontrolled conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration, abnormal psychological state, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes are clinically associated with acute kidney failure. Acute kidney failure often develops in the context of severe dehydration, which can precipitate rapid loss of renal function. Patients with acute kidney failure may also present with abnormal psychological states, reflecting the systemic impact of renal dysfunction on mental status. Moreover, type 2 diabetes that requires insulin therapy is linked to acute kidney failure, indicating that poorly controlled diabetes is a significant comorbidity that can predispose to or worsen renal injury. Recognizing these interconnections aids in comprehensive assessment and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2839
retrieved_docs.content: بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes). Patients with type 1 diabetes have a heightened risk of severe peripheral vascular disease and foot complications, which can progress to the need for an above‑knee amputation. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians to implement early screening, aggressive glycemic control, and multidisciplinary foot‑care strategies aimed at preventing major limb loss and improving overall patient outcomes.
